segments.title: Why journalists can use anonymous sources but historians cannot
segments.summary: Journalists can vet protected sources while reporting urgent events, whereas historians must leave an evidence trail that another scholar can inspect and test.
segments.body: Journalists often cover events while they are unfolding, when the public needs information and an informed source may speak only if protected from professional or personal consequences. The reporter can meet that person, ask questions, compare claims, and decide whether the source is trustworthy even when the public cannot see the source's identity. Historians work under a different evidentiary requirement. They must show a chain of sources that another scholar can follow and use to test the factual basis of the argument. An anonymous source whose identity and context cannot be recovered breaks that reproducible chain. The difference is therefore between the demands of timely reporting and those of independently checkable historical analysis, not between verification and a lack of verification.
segments.question: Why can journalists use anonymous sources when historians generally cannot?
segments.answerShort: A journalist can personally assess a protected source during an urgent event, but a historian must provide an evidence trail that later researchers can independently follow and substantiate.

evidence.note: Dr Clarke explains why immediate reporting may depend on protected sources whom a journalist can assess directly.
He contrasts that practice with the historian's duty to provide a source trail another scholar can follow and substantiate.

segments.title: Anonymous newspaper sources turn historians into detectives
segments.summary: Anonymous attribution in contemporary newspapers can force historians to reconstruct who supplied a claim before treating the report as dependable evidence.
segments.body: A newspaper article from the period does not automatically solve the historian's source problem if its decisive information came from an unnamed person. Without knowing who spoke, the researcher cannot readily judge access, motive, or reliability. The historian may therefore have to reconstruct who could have leaked the information and which journalist or paper received it. Dr Clarke describes work with early twentieth-century, 1920s, and 1930s newspapers as a form of historical detective work because their wording often tries to conceal an obvious source. Demanding that reconstruction complicates research, but it also makes the resulting analysis easier for other historians to substantiate.

evidence.note: He explains why anonymously sourced contemporary journalism remains difficult for historians to trust.
He describes the benefits of substantiation and the detective work required by newspapers from the early twentieth century through the 1930s.
The conclusion ties this evidentiary problem to historians' different treatment of sources.
